content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

PILLSBURY, MADISON & SUTRO
POST OFFICE BOX 7880

OTHER OFFICES

LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA
WALNUT CREEK, CALIFORNIA
WASHINGTON, D.C.

SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94120

TELEPHONE (415) 983-1000

CABLE ADDRESS
PILLSUB
TELEX 34743
TELECOPIER (415) 398-2096

WRITER'S OFFICE AND
DIRECT DIAL NUMBER

235 Montgomery Street
(415) 983-1585

August 31, 1990


VIA RAPIFAX

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Jeanne:

     Pursuant to my recent conversations with you and
Carla Wardlow, this is a request for written advice pursuant
to Government Code § 83114(b).

     This firm represents the Pacific Telesis Group
(the "Company"). The Company is contemplating sponsoring
appearances by the Democratic and Republican candidates for
Governor of California, which will be attended by the
Company's employees at the Company's facilities.

     The Political Reform Act of 1974, as amended (the
"Act"), defines a contribution or expenditure to include any
payment unless it is clear from the surrounding circum-
stances that it is not made for "political purposes." See
Gov't Code §§ 82015 and 82025. The term "political pur-
poses" is defined by the Commission's regulations to mean
"...made for the purpose of influencing or attempting to
influence the action of voters for or against the...election
of a candidate...." 2 Cal.Code Regs. §§ 18215(a)(1) and
18225(a)(1). The Commission has advised that a corporation
may invite all announced candidates for an office to speak
to its employees and that the expenses associated therewith
are not in-kind contributions so long as the event is non-
partisan. See Lippert advice letter (No. A-87-172); see
also, Polak advice letter (No. A-77-03-003) and Del Campo
advice letter (No. A-77-04-010).

     The Company plans to invite the Republican and
Democratic candidates for Governor to speak on separate
days. The Company intends to make every effort to ensure

1043594l

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
August 31, 1990
Page 2

that no candidate will be given any preferential treatment.
The same facilities will be made available to each candidate and each will be given the same amount of time to
express his or her views. The event is contemplated solely
as a civic service to the Company's employees. The candidates will not be afforded an opportunity to solicit
campaign contributions, and the Company will not advocate
the election or defeat of either candidate.

Based on our conversations, we understand the
following to be the Commission's position on this type of
event:

1. Unless all candidates for an office who will
appear on the ballot are invited to participate, including
those nominated by minor political parties, the sponsor of
the event will be deemed to have made contributions in-kind
to the campaigns of the candidates who appear.

2. Even if all of the candidates, including
those nominated by minor political parties, are invited to
participate, the candidates must all speak at the same time
in the presence of one another in a forum-type setting. If
they appear separately on different days, then contributions
in-kind by the sponsor will result.

3. Expenses incurred by the Company in connection with each appearance may be exempted from the
definition of contribution pursuant to Government Code
§ 82015. Specifically, the exception for office meetings
contained in Gov't Code § 82015 will apply to the events
proposed by the Company, provided that the entire cost
associated with each speech does not exceed the $500
threshold provided therein. In other words, in this instance, the Company would be deemed to be "an occupant" of
its own facilities and therefore would be entitled to the
$500 exception.

Assuming that the foregoing is thus far a correct
summary of the Commission's interpretation of the law as
applied to this situation, it then becomes necessary to
determine which "costs" count toward the $500 threshold. In
that connection, we note that regular ongoing business overhead should not count toward this threshold (see 2 FPPC Ops.
133, 138 (1976)). Please confirm your advice that in this
instance none of the following uses of Company facilities,
equipment or personnel will result in a "cost" which (1)
must be counted toward the $500 threshold in § 82015 and (2)

10435941

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
August 31, 1990
Page 3

if the threshold is exceeded, must be treated as a "con-
tribution" under the Act:

a. The speeches are held in an auditorium or
meeting room that is used by the Company for similar events
but is also made available to the public after hours for a
rental fee.

b. The speeches are held in an auditorium or
meeting room that is regularly used by the Company for such
events but is also made available to the public at no
charge.

c. The speeches are held in an auditorium or
meeting room that is regularly used by the Company for such
events but is never made available to the public.

d. Equipment (such as furniture, microphones,
etc.) is used in conjunction with the speeches. The
Company already owns all the necessary equipment; that is,
no equipment is purchased or leased for the events.

e. Employees assist in the preparations for the
events. No employees involved (including attendees) spend
more than 10% of their compensated time during any one month
rendering services for political purposes (see 2 Cal. Code
Regs. 18423).

The Company intends to announce these events to
its employees and encourage their attendance. That
announcement may be made in a regularly published weekly
bulletin or newsletter which is sent to all employees and
which meets the criteria of 2 Cal. Code Regs. § 18215(b)
(4)(C). We understand, however, that it is the Commission's
position that, while the proportional cost associated with
this newsletter announcement is not subject to Proposition
73 limits, it may nevertheless count toward the $500 thresh-
old. This is because the Commission does not believe that
the so-called "newsletter exception" applies to contribu-
tions as defined in 2 Cal. Code Regs. § 18215 (notwith-
standing the policy of 2 FPPC Ops. 133, supra). Moreover,
special bulletins (which the Company occasionally circulates
to announce special occurrences or important developments
which are distributed to announce the speaking events would
be treated similarly.

Finally, the costs of any refreshments served at
the events will count toward the $500 threshold, and if the

10435941

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
August 31, 1990
Page 4


threshold is exceeded, must be treated as contributions in-
kind to the respective candidates.

     We appreciate your assistance in this matter.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Frederick K. Lowell

cc:  Ms. Carla Wardlow




10435941
